title: NIS2 Risk Analysis Evidence for Essential Entities
exclusions: Does not classify the entity's NIS2 scope (essential vs important), interpret national transposition thresholds, or replace counsel determination of Annex I/II applicability. Only surfaces telemetry-based risk evidence that an auditor can review against a pre-classified scope.
evidence: Scheduled run of `UC-22.2.21` with timestamp, field extract snapshot (`risk_object`, `analysis_stale`), and PDF dashboard export retained in `audit_evidence` for essential-entity risk monitoring.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.21(2) is assigned contributing assurance because this UC provides concrete Splunk evidence for the observable control condition, while policy approval, legal interpretation, and official filings remain outside Splunk.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIS2 Art.21(2) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.2.21: NIS2 Risk Analysis Evidence for Essential Entities.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.2.21' running on index=risk sourcetype="stash", tenable:vuln, nis2_entity_classification.csv (entity_tier=essential), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Load a test `risk_object=E-TEST-01` in `nis2_entity_classification.csv` with `entity_tier=essential`, inject `grc:risk` rows where `risk_score=90`, and confirm `analysis_stale=ESCALATE` even if vulnerability count is zero.
controlTest.negativeScenario: For the same asset, lower `risk_score` below 50 and ensure active critical findings stay at or below policy so the row drops off when rerunning after the lookback windows refresh with clean data.

dataSources: `index=grc` `sourcetype=grc:risk` with `risk_object`, `risk_score`, `review_date`, `grc_status`; `index=vuln` `sourcetype=tenable:vuln` with `host`, `plugin_id`, `severity`, `state` (Tenable TA Splunkbase 4060). Policy cadence from `index=itsm` `sourcetype=snow:sn_grc_policy` (`policy_number`, `next_review_date`) via Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928). In-scope essential assets come from lookup `nis2_entity_classification.csv` fields `asset_id`, `business_service`, `entity_tier`. Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263) enriches identities when `host` maps to `assets_by_str`.
app: Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263), Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928), Tenable Add-On for Splunk (Splunkbase 4060)
spl:
| inputlookup nis2_entity_classification.csv WHERE entity_tier="essential"
| rename asset_id as risk_object
| join risk_object type=left [
    search index=grc sourcetype=grc:risk earliest=-90d
    | stats latest(risk_score) as residual_risk max(risk_score) as peak_risk by risk_object
]
| join risk_object type=left [
    search index=vuln sourcetype="tenable:vuln" severity IN ("Critical","High") state="Active" earliest=-14d
    | stats dc(plugin_id) as open_critical_high by host
    | rename host as risk_object
]
| fillnull value=0 residual_risk peak_risk open_critical_high
| eval analysis_stale=if(residual_risk > 80 OR open_critical_high > 5, "ESCALATE", "Monitor")
| table risk_object, business_service, residual_risk, peak_risk, open_critical_high, analysis_stale
| sort - residual_risk
description: Correlates GRC risk scores and live high-severity exposure for assets classified as essential, highlighting combinations where analysis is stale or backlog exceeds policy so essential-tier proof stays current.
value: Lets executives defend the essential-entity risk register during supervision by showing machine-generated joins between formal scores, open scanner reality, and the legal tier tag—without relying on quarterly PowerPoint snapshots alone.
implementation: (1) Keep `nis2_entity_classification.csv` authoritative for `entity_tier=essential` and map `asset_id` to scanner host names. (2) Land `grc:risk` nightly with stable `risk_object` keys. (3) Save search `UC-22.2.21` tri-weekly; page risk owners only on `analysis_stale=ESCALATE`. (4) Pair with ServiceNow GRC tasks for remediation links. (5) Archive outputs with `evidenceSigning` when submitting annual packs.
visualization: Heatmap of residual risk by `business_service`, sortable table of essential assets breaching thresholds, single-value average residual risk, 90-day trend line.
knownFalsePositives:
• **Accepted risk entries not coded in GRC** — Distinguish: residual numbers stay high because formal acceptance exists yet Splunk lacks the flag. Suppress: join `nis2_risk_exception_register.csv` on `risk_object` where `exception_state=approved`.
• **Scanner duplicates from dual agents** — Distinguish: `open_critical_high` inflates when two Tenable connectors report the same host. Suppress: dedup inside a macro `m_tenable_host_dedup` before `dc(plugin_id)`.
• **Essential asset hostname drift** — Distinguish: CMDB retired `asset_id` but scanners still use old hostname. Suppress: lookup `nis2_asset_alias.csv` mapping `legacy_host`→`risk_object`.
• **Quarterly GRC import lag** — Distinguish: `grc:risk` timestamps stall while manual workshops finish. Suppress: time-bound exception rows valid **30 days** with owner approval in `nis2_exception_register.csv`.
• **Decommissioned plant still in scanner** — Distinguish: host pings but tag `decomm=true` in CMDB. Suppress: filter `| lookup nis2_decom_hosts.csv host OUTPUT decomm | where decomm!="true"`.
equipmentModels: ServiceNow GRC/IRM module (risk scoring, risk object mapping to CMDB CIs)
Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928, GRC risk register REST input)
Splunk Enterprise Security 7.x (Splunkbase 263, asset model and identity resolution)
Tenable Add-On for Splunk (Splunkbase 4060, vulnerability detection exports)
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Technology Add-on**: Tenable Add-on for Splunk (Splunkbase app ID 4060) must be installed on search heads and indexers. Verify with `| rest /services/apps/local | search label="Tenable Add-on for Splunk" | table label version`.
• **Indexes**: `grc`, `vuln` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Lookups**: `nis2_entity_classification.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('nis2_entity_classification.csv')`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.21(2)`. The control objective is: "Evidence that NIS2 Art.21(2) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.2.21: NIS2 Risk Analysis Evidence for Essential Entities." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is performed by the Tenable Add-on for Splunk (Splunkbase app ID 4060), landing events in `grc`, `vuln` under `grc:risk`, `tenable:vuln`. Daily vulnerability deltas for a 10000-asset estate produce 50000-500000 findings/day (~5-50 GB/month). Full snapshots weekly add more.

Configure the input through Settings > Data Inputs > Tenable.sc Vulnerability. A reference `inputs.conf` stanza looks like:

```ini
[tenable_sc_vuln://prod]
endpoint = https://tenable.example.com
interval = 86400
index = vulnerability
sourcetype = tenable:sc:vuln
```

The following fields are authoritative for this detection once the TA parses them: `pluginID, severity, state, first_seen, last_seen, host, dns, ip, plugin_name, cve`. Verify they are extracted at search time with `index=grc earliest=-15m | fieldsummary` before scheduling the detection.


5 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Keep `nis2_entity_classification.csv` authoritative for `entity_tier=essential` and map `asset_id` to scanner host names. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** Land `grc:risk` nightly with stable `risk_object` keys. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** Save search `UC-22.2.21` tri-weekly; page risk owners only on `analysis_stale=ESCALATE`. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** Pair with ServiceNow GRC tasks for remediation links. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 5:** Archive outputs with `evidenceSigning` when submitting annual packs. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "NIS2 Risk Analysis Evidence for Essential Entities," the aggregation is the evidence artifact itself — auditors will read these rows as the official record of NIS2 control activity.

This search begins with `| inputlookup nis2_entity_classification.csv` — the base dataset is a CSV/KV Store lookup, not live event data. This inverts the usual Splunk assumption: the detection does not stream from an index, it iterates a register. The register itself is the authoritative source of truth (contracts, policies, sign-offs, attestations) and the search joins it against indexed events to find gaps.

Lookup authority: treat `nis2_entity_classification.csv` as a primary compliance artifact. Its contents ARE the answer the regulator will ask for ('show me your vendor list,' 'show me your CDE inventory,' 'show me your policy exceptions'). Implement the following controls on the lookup itself, not just the search:

• Version the CSV in a git repository with a mandatory pull-request review for every change.
• Maintain a signed-record trail: who changed what, when, and with what approval. A lookup with a silent edit history is an audit liability.
• Automate a nightly diff: `| inputlookup nis2_entity_classification.csv | eval hash=md5(...) | outputlookup append=true nis2_entity_classification_audit.csv` — this produces an immutable record of the register's state over time.
• Lock the lookup with role-based access: only the content owner has `list_inputs` + `write_file` on the path; everyone else is read-only.

The live-data side of the join (across the last 90 days) is the detection trigger — when the register and the telemetry disagree, that's a finding.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.2.21: NIS2 Risk Analysis Evidence for Essential Entities]
cron_schedule = 0 3 1 * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -90d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.2.21,reg=NIS2
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=grc sourcetype=grc:risk earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `analysis_stale`, `business_service`, `open_critical_high`, `peak_risk`, `residual_risk`, `risk_object`. Run:

```spl
index=grc sourcetype=grc:risk earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table analysis_stale, business_service, open_critical_high, peak_risk, residual_risk, risk_object
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(d) Threshold calibration:** Before enabling as a production alert, run the base search with the threshold removed and profile the distribution of `residual_risk`:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats perc50(residual_risk) as p50 perc90(residual_risk) as p90 perc99(residual_risk) as p99 max(residual_risk) as hi
```

The current threshold `80` should sit between p90 and p99 for a balanced signal-to-noise ratio. If it's below p50, expect heavy alert volume; if above p99, expect under-detection.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.2.21: NIS2 evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Heatmap Of Residual Risk By `Business Service` (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Sortable Table Of Essential Assets Breaching Thresholds (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — Single-Value Average Residual Risk (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 4 — 90-Day Trend Line (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Search returns no results when violations are expected:** Verify time-window alignment, index existence, and field extractions in that order. Most 'missing results' are simpler configuration issues before they're detection-logic issues.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**NIS2-specific failure modes:**

• 24/7 reporting obligation (Art. 23): NIS2 requires early warning within 24 hours of awareness of a significant incident. Detections that back this SLA must compute elapsed time from the SOC's first-acknowledged timestamp (not the resolve-time) and must trigger independently of business hours — the regulatory clock does not pause overnight.

• Member-state variation: NIS2 is transposed individually by each EU Member State with some latitude on thresholds and sector scoping. A detection correct for German IT-SiG 3 (BSI) may be misaligned with Dutch CWV or French LPM. Use `nis2_member_state_config.csv` to parametrize thresholds per jurisdiction; never hardcode.

• Essential vs Important entity criteria: the applicable obligations depend on sector + size. Before alerting, verify the monitored entity's designation; a detection firing on an out-of-scope subsidiary causes unnecessary regulatory notifications that erode trust with the national CSIRT.

• Supply-chain (Art. 21(2)(d)) extent: NIS2 explicitly includes direct suppliers' cybersecurity posture. Detections must cover monitored-by-you-not-owned-by-you infrastructure (outsourced SaaS, managed SIEM tenants) — these are in scope even if operationally run by a third party. BA/BAA-style governance applies.
grandmaExplanation: We line up the official "how risky is this asset" score with what the vulnerability scanner still sees for the sites labelled most important, so weaker spots do not hide behind paperwork.
